items.q: What does it mean that RAM is volatile?
items.correct: Its contents are lost when power is removed.
items.misconception: It can explode or be damaged easily.
items.why: Students take 'volatile' from chemistry literally. In computing, volatile means contents don't persist without power - that's why RAM is used for working memory, not storage.

items.q: What does an INNER JOIN return?
items.correct: Only rows with matching values in both tables.
items.misconception: All rows from both tables combined.
items.random_wrong: Only the inner columns of each table.
items.why: Students confuse INNER (intersection) with FULL OUTER (union). INNER drops unmatched rows from BOTH sides; LEFT keeps all from left; RIGHT keeps all from right; FULL OUTER keeps everything.

items.correct: Bundling data and methods together while restricting direct access to internal state.
items.random_wrong: Encrypting data so it cannot be read.
items.why: Students take 'capsule' too literally. Encapsulation combines two ideas: keeping data and behaviour TOGETHER, and HIDING implementation behind a public interface.
The answer examiners want is: RAM. “ROM” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which storage has no moving parts?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: SSD. “HDD” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many bytes are in 1 kilobyte (decimal)?
items.why: Exact answer: 1000 bytes. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
